summary: The translation preserves all Greek, Hebrew, Latin, and German spans intact and correctly applies the glossary term for the section title. The English prose is generally coherent formal theological language, but several passages exhibit awkward syntax from overly literal rendering of Dutch constructions, particularly passive formations and inverted word order. No doctrinal ambiguities or preservation corruptions were detected.

preserved-language-integrity.details: All Greek, Hebrew, Latin, and German spans from the source appear unchanged in the translation.

glossary-adherence.details: Section title 'INDEELING DER NAMEN GODS' correctly rendered as 'CLASSIFICATION OF THE NAMES OF GOD' per glossary.

prose-quality.details: Formal theological prose maintained throughout; argument structure preserved. Minor syntactic awkwardness noted but does not flatten meaning.

review-flagging.details: Findings below identify specific prose quality issues for follow-up.

findings.detail: Awkward passive constructions: 'there is spoken there of the being or nature of God' and 'In the confessions it is also often spoken of God in that manner' mirror Dutch syntax rather than natural English word order.

Inverted word order in 'Against this cold, moralistic concept of God came again reaction from the side of philosophy' and 'Schelling distinguished himself from both thereby, that he reckoned' impedes readability.
'God was equal to him to the pure ego' in the Fichte passage is confusing; clearer rendering would be 'God was for him identical with the pure ego.'

recommended_follow_up: Revise passive constructions to natural English word order where Dutch syntax has been carried over.
Restructure sentences with inverted or awkward word order for smoother theological prose.
Clarify the Fichte passage to ensure the identification of God with the pure ego is unambiguous.
